overview.description: A dialog is a type of modal window that appears in front of app content to provide critical information or ask for a decision.
overview.purpose: A dialog is a type of modal window that appears in front of app content to provide critical information or ask for a decision.
whenToUse: When you need to display important information or ask for user input.
whenNotToUse: When you need to display non-critical information or when a simple alert would suffice.

examples.basic: <mc-dialog heading="Heading" body="A question prompting the user to make a decision?" actions="" fit="medium" dimension="medium" width="" zindex="99999" autofocus=""></mc-dialog>

Vue3.basic:
<mc-dialog
  heading="Heading"
  body="A question prompting the user to make a decision?"
  fit="small"
  dimension="small"
  width="value"
  open
  escapecloseactiondisabled
  nonmodal
  nopadding
  disablestickyfooter
  @closed="yourEventHandler(e)"
  @closing="yourEventHandler(e)"
  @opened="yourEventHandler(e)"
  @opening="yourEventHandler(e)"
  @click="yourEventHandler(e)"
  hasheadingslot
  hasbodyslot
  hasfooterslot>
</mc-dialog>

VanillaJs.minimal:
<script type="module"
  src="./node_modules/@maersk-global/mds-components-core-dialog/index.js"></script>

<mc-dialog>
</mc-dialog>

<script>
  document.querySelector('mc-dialog').addEventListener('closed', (e) => yourEventHandler(e));
  document.querySelector('mc-dialog').addEventListener('closing', (e) => yourEventHandler(e));
  document.querySelector('mc-dialog').addEventListener('opened', (e) => yourEventHandler(e));
  document.querySelector('mc-dialog').addEventListener('opening', (e) => yourEventHandler(e));
  document.querySelector('mc-dialog').addEventListener('click', (e) => yourEventHandler(e));
</script>

props.name: heading
props.description: No heading will be shown, if attribute, or heading slot is not supplied

props.name: body
props.defaultValue: A question prompting the user to make a decision?
props.description: Body can be provided as the default slot i.e.:`<mc-dialog>rich text</mc-dialog>` or as a string attribute.

props.name: actions
props.description: There are two named slots available: `primaryAction` & `secondaryAction`. You can pass into those i.e.: confirm or cancel action, like: ```<mc-button slot="primaryAction" dialogaction="ok">OK</mc-button>``` or ```<mc-button slot="secondaryAction" dialogaction="cancel">Cancel</mc-button>``` For more details on usage, please look at code snippet in the example above

props.name: dimension
props.description:
On the small screens dialog will be shown in full-screen size, regardless of the dialog dimension.
      
The specifications of each size preset are as below:
      
- small: `width: 320px, height: auto` 
      
- medium: `width: 480px, height: auto` 
      
- large: `width: 800px, height: auto`

props.name: width
props.description:
Sets the width of the component.
    You can also set the width for different breakpoints by passing an object with the breakpoint sizes i.e.

    { 
    xs: '100%',
    sm: '400px',
    md: '500px',
    lg: '600px',
    xl: '700px' 
    }

    
    Remember also to specify the measurement unit i.e. `px`, `%` or `vw`, etc.

props.name: zindex
props.description: Should only be set if two dialogs are supposed to be shown on top of each other

props.name: escapecloseactiondisabled
props.description: Disables the `Escape` key from closing the dialog.

props.name: nonmodal
props.description: If true, the dialog will open as a non-modal dialog, allowing page interaction.

props.name: nopadding
props.description: If true, the dialog content will have no padding.

props.name: disablestickyfooter
props.description: If true, the dialog footer will not be sticky.

props.name: autofocus
props.description: Add `autofocus` attiribute to an element inside of dialog to set focus on it when dialog is opened

props.name: hasheadingslot
props.description:
**For server-side rendering only (e.g., Next.js).**


Set to true when providing content to the heading slot. Not needed for client-side rendering as the slot is detected automatically.

props.name: hasbodyslot
props.description:
**For server-side rendering only (e.g., Next.js).**


Set to true when providing content to the default body slot. Not needed for client-side rendering as the slot is detected automatically.

props.name: hasfooterslot
props.description:
**For server-side rendering only (e.g., Next.js).**


Set to true when providing content to footer/action slots. Not needed for client-side rendering as the slot is detected automatically.

events.name: closed
events.description: The closed event gets dispatched once the dialog is closed.

events.name: closing
events.description: The closing event gets dispatched once the dialog is closing.

events.name: opened
events.description: The opened event gets dispatched once the dialog is opened.

events.name: opening
events.description: The opening event gets dispatched once the dialog is opening.

events.name: click
events.description: The click event gets dispatched when clicked on the primaryAction/secondaryAction button within a dialog.

slots.description: Use the default slot, when you want to pass body as HTML to the dialog. Check stories under Examples folder to see live example.
Use the primaryAction named slot, when you want to pass confirm action button. Check stories under Examples folder to see live example.
slots.name: primaryAction

slots.description: Use the secondaryAction named slot, when you want to pass cancel action button. Check stories under Examples folder to see live example.
slots.name: secondaryAction

types.name: McDialogAction
types.jsdoc: Indicates the action that was taken when the dialog was closed.

cssParts.description: Using this CSS part you can change the look of the the dialog element. Check Examples -> Customised appearance story to see live example.
cssParts.name: dialog

cssParts.description: Using this CSS part you can change the look of the header of the dialog. Check Examples -> Customised appearance story to see live example.
cssParts.name: header

cssParts.description: Using this CSS part you can change the look of the wrapper of the dialog content. Check Examples -> Customised appearance story to see live example.
cssParts.name: body-wrapper

cssParts.description: Using this CSS part you can change the look of the footer of the dialog. Check Examples -> Customised appearance story to see live example.
cssParts.name: footer

guidelines.title: Dialog
guidelines.description: A Dialog is a disruptive overlay that requires the user to make a decision before proceeding further.

best-practice: Dialogs are inherently disruptive, so use them sparingly.
Use dialogs to display messages of high-urgency.
Use dialogs in cases that require a decision or acknowledgement from the user.
Keep the dialog messages short and concise.
Avoid ambigious and vague copy to minimise mistakes.
placement: Dialogs are placed in the centre of the viewport and on top of everything else in the UI, this is done to ensure the user's full attention. While the dialog is active, a backdrop (scrim) is rendered underneath and the rest of the UI is inaccessible until the user clicks on one of the actions in the dialog or dismisses it by pressing ESC.
To see the dialog with the backdrop, click on the "Click to see dialog" button in the demo section in the top of the page.

designPrinciples.title: Spacing
designPrinciples.description: Consistent spacing creates groups, rhythm and good scan-ability.

use-4-pixel-spacing: The base unit for spacing is 4 pixels, subsequent units are multiples of 4 (such as 8, 12, 16, etc.). These units should be used consistently for all paddings, margins, gutters, typography, and layouts to ensure a cohesive appearance and user experience across all Maersk digital products.
exceptions-to-4-pixel-spacing: When creating components, finer control is needed for spacing elements. Three acceptable exceptions here are 2, 6, and 10 pixels.
components: Relate the component elements and still keep them distinct by using space units dervided from the 4-pixel base spacing. These space units will make aligning and using non-core components with core ones easier.
patterns: A recognisable pattern is created when consistent spacing is used to establish a visual rhythm and relationship between the pattern elements.

Pattern spacing needs to be coherent which means not all space units need to be the same. Several spacing units can help establish better visual balance when dealing with differently-sized UI elements in the pattern.
layouts: Visual hierarchy in layouts requires well spaced regions and UI elements. Spacing units can control the information density of any application layout. Use them to provide the right level for the context and users' expectation.
information-density: Space is the element that controls the information density of any interface. Adjusting the space between blocks on information creates a visual rhythm and hierarchy.

High information density can be disorienting and overwhelming if spacing does not allow the eyes to rest and let people process what they see. People may notice the grouped elements but not process each individual item.

We recommend reading the <PageLink path="/design-language/component-fit/">Fit guidelines</PageLink> to help you make an informed decision what spacing units will be most suitable for your layout composition.

relatedGuidelines.title: Accessibility
relatedGuidelines.description: Improving the user experience for everyone.

what-is-accessibility?: Accessibility means that websites, tools and technologies are designed and developed in a way that makes it possible for people with disabilities to use them. Accessibility also benefits people without disabilities for example people with temporary disabilities or situational disabilities.
Accessibility helps people perceive, understand and interact with our digital products and it takes auditory, cognitive, neurological, physical, speech and visual limitations into consideration.

But accessibility is also a mindset and a change in the way we think and talk about digital products. It is a cultural change that cannot be made by one team only. We, therefore, urge all our colleagues as well as top management to get involved and share awareness about this very important topic. We need your support!
why-is-accessibility-important?: One of the main myths about accessibility is that it only concerns a small group of people with severe disabilities. This assumption is far from the reality and according to the World Health Organisation (WHO), more than 1 billion people are living with some level of impairment that affects the way they interact with digital products and access information online.
When our websites, apps and other digital products are designed badly, they create barriers that exclude people and make access to our offerings cumbersome and in some cases not possible at all.
being-inaccessible-is-a-problem: It is an ethical problem when we exclude certain groups of people from our products and services.

It is a legal problem, especially in the US where companies are being sued if they are not accessible, which could have financial consequences in terms of fines as well as affecting the brand negatively.

It is a big business problem - if we choose to exclude a very big group of potential customers because they have a disability.
When we design and build products with accessibility in mind, we not only open up our products and experiences for more people, but our products start to become more human - in the sense that they reflect the diversity of society and become much more flexible and robust to accommodate many different needs and use cases.
